html:
XVII

Armand and Olivier

That same evening, while Edouard was talking to his nephew George, Olivier, after Bernard had left him, received a visit from Armand.

Armand Vedel was unrecognizable; shaved, smiling, carrying his head high; he was dressed in a new suit, which was rather too smart and looked perhaps a trifle ridiculous; he felt it and showed that he felt it.

‚ÄúI should have come to see you before, but I‚Äôve had so much to do lately!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Do you know that I‚Äôve actually become Passavant‚Äôs secretary? or, if you prefer it, the editor of his new review. I won‚Äôt ask you to contribute, because Passavant seems rather worked up against you. Besides the review is decidedly going more and more to the left. That‚Äôs the reason it has begun by dropping Bercail and his pastorals.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm sorry for the review,‚Äù said Olivier.

‚ÄúAnd that‚Äôs why, on the other hand, it has accepted my Nocturnal Vase, which, by the by, is, without your permission, to be dedicated to you.‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm sorry for me.‚Äù

‚ÄúPassavant even wished my work of genius to open the first number; but my natural modesty, which was severely tried by his encomiums, was opposed to this. If I were not afraid of fatiguing a convalescent‚Äôs ears, I would give you an account of my first interview with the illustrious author of The Horizontal Bar, whom I had only known up till then through you.‚Äù

‚ÄúI have nothing better to do than to listen.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt mind smoke?‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôll smoke myself to show you.‚Äù

‚ÄúI must tell you,‚Äù began Armand, lighting a cigarette, ‚Äúthat your desertion left our beloved Count somewhat in a fix. Let it be said, without flattery, that it isn‚Äôt easy to replace such a bundle of gifts, virtues, qualities as are united in your.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúGet on,‚Äù interrupted Olivier, exasperated by this heavy-footed irony.

‚ÄúWell, to get on, Passavant wanted a secretary. He happened to know a certain Strouvilhou, whom I happen to know myself, because he is the uncle of a certain individual in the school, who happened to know Jean Cob-Lafleur, whom you know.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhom I don‚Äôt know,‚Äù said Olivier.

‚ÄúWell, my boy, you ought to know him. He‚Äôs an extraordinary fellow; a kind of faded, wrinkled, painted baby, who lives on cocktails and writes charming verses when he‚Äôs drunk. You‚Äôll see some in our first number. So Strouvilhou had the brilliant idea of sending him to Passavant, to take your place. You can imagine his entry into the Rue de Babylone mansion. I must tell you that Cob-Lafleur‚Äôs clothes are covered with stains; that he has flowing flaxen locks, which fall upon his shoulders; and that he looks as if he hadn‚Äôt washed for a week. Passavant, who always wants to be master of the situation, declares that he took a great fancy to Cob-Lafleur. Cob-Lafleur has a gentle, smiling, timid way with him. When he chooses he can look like Banville‚Äôs Gringoire. In a word, Passavant was taken by him and was on the point of engaging him. I must tell you that Lafleur hasn‚Äôt got a penny piece.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ So he gets up to take leave:‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòBefore leaving, Monsieur le Comte, I think it‚Äôs only right to inform you that I have a few faults.‚Äô‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòWhich of us has not?‚Äô‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòAnd a few vices. I smoke opium.‚Äô‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòIs that all?‚Äô says Passavant, who isn‚Äôt to be put off by a little thing of that kind; ‚ÄòI‚Äôve got some excellent stuff to offer you.‚Äô‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòYes, but when I smoke it, I completely lose every notion of spelling.‚Äô Passavant took this for a joke, forced a laugh and held out his hand. Lafleur goes on:‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòAnd then I take hashish.‚Äô‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòI have sometimes taken it myself,‚Äô says Passavant.‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòYes, but when I am under the influence of hashish, I can‚Äôt keep from stealing.‚Äô Passavant began to see then that he was being made a fool of; and Lafleur, who was set going by now, rattled on, impulsively:‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòAnd besides, I drink ether; and then I tear everything to bits‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI smash everything I can lay my hands on,‚Äô and he seizes a glass vase and makes as if he were going to throw it into the fire. Passavant just had time to snatch it out of his hands.‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòMuch obliged to you for warning me.‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd he chucked him out?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes; and watched out of the window to see Lafleur didn‚Äôt drop a bomb into the cellar as he left.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut why did Lafleur behave so? From what you say, he was really in need of the place.‚Äù

‚ÄúAll the same, my dear fellow, you must admit that there are people who feel impelled to act against their interest. And then, if you want to know, Lafleur‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ well, Passavant‚Äôs luxury disgusted him‚ÅÝ‚Äîhis elegance, his amiable manners, his condescension, his affectation of superiority. Yes; it turned his stomach. And I add that I perfectly understand him.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ At bottom, your Passavant makes one‚Äôs gorge rise.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy do you say ‚Äòyour Passavant‚Äô? You know quite well that I‚Äôve given him up. And then why have you accepted his place, if you think him so disgusting?‚Äù

‚ÄúFor the very reason that I like things that disgust me‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ to start with my own delightful‚ÅÝ‚Äîor disgusting‚ÅÝ‚Äîself. And then, in reality, Cob-Lafleur suffers from shyness; he wouldn‚Äôt have said any of all that if he hadn‚Äôt felt ill at ease.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh! come now!‚Äù

‚ÄúCertainly. He was ill at ease, and he was furious at being made to feel ill at ease by someone he really despises. It was to conceal his shyness that he bluffed.‚Äù

‚ÄúI call it stupid.‚Äù

‚ÄúMy dear fellow, everyone can‚Äôt be as intelligent as you are.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou said that last time, too.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat a memory!‚Äù

Olivier was determined to hold his ground.

‚ÄúI try,‚Äù said he, ‚Äúto forget your jokes. But last time you did at last talk to me seriously. You said things I can‚Äôt forget.‚Äù

Armand‚Äôs eyes grew troubled. He went off into a forced laugh.

‚ÄúOh, old fellow, last time I talked to you as you wanted to be talked to. You called for something in a minor key, so, in order to please you, I played my lament, with a soul like a corkscrew and anguish √Ý la Pascal.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ It can‚Äôt be helped, you know. I‚Äôm only sincere when I‚Äôm cracking jokes.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou‚Äôll never make me believe that you weren‚Äôt sincere when you talked to me as you did that day. It‚Äôs now that you are playing a part.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, simplicity! What a pure angelic soul you possess! As if we weren‚Äôt all playing parts more or less sincerely and consciously. Life, my dear fellow, is nothing but a comedy. But the difference between you and me is that I know I am playing a part, whilst.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúWhilst‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù repeated Olivier aggressively.

‚ÄúWhilst my father, for instance, not to speak of you, is completely taken in when he plays at being a pastor. Whatever I say or do, there‚Äôs always one part of myself which stays behind, and watches the other part compromise itself, which laughs at and hisses it, or applauds it. When one is divided in that way, how is it possible to be sincere? I have got to the point of ceasing to understand what the word means. It can‚Äôt be helped; when I‚Äôm sad, I seem so grotesque to myself that it makes me laugh; when I‚Äôm cheerful, I make such idiotic jokes that I feel inclined to cry.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou make me feel inclined to cry too, my dear boy. I didn‚Äôt think you were in such a bad way.‚Äù

Armand shrugged his shoulders and went on in a totally different tone of voice:

‚ÄúTo console you, should you like to know the contents of our first number? Well, there‚Äôs my Nocturnal Vase; four songs by Cob-Lafleur; a dialogue by Jarry; some prose poems by young Gh√©ridanisol, one of our boarders; and then The Flat Iron, a vast essay in general criticism, in which the tendencies of the review will be more or less definitely laid down. Several of us have combined together to produce this chef d‚Äôoeuvre.‚Äù

Olivier, not knowing what to say, objected clumsily:

‚ÄúNo chef d‚Äôoeuvre was ever produced by several people together.‚Äù

Armand burst out laughing:

‚ÄúBut, my dear fellow, I said it was a chef d‚Äôoeuvre as a joke. It isn‚Äôt a chef d‚Äôoeuvre; it isn‚Äôt anything at all. And, for that matter, what does one mean by chef d‚Äôoeuvre? That‚Äôs just what The Flat Iron tries to get to the bottom of. There are heaps of works one admires on faith, just because everyone else does, and because no one so far has thought of saying‚ÅÝ‚Äîor dared to say‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat they were stupid. For instance, on the first page of this number, we are going to give a reproduction of the Mona Lisa, with a pair of moustaches stuck on to her face. You‚Äôll see! The effect is simply staggering.‚Äù

‚ÄúDoes that mean you consider the Mona Lisa a stupidity?‚Äù

‚ÄúNot at all, my dear fellow. (Though I don‚Äôt think it as marvellous as all that.) You don‚Äôt understand me. The thing that‚Äôs stupid is people‚Äôs admiration for it. It‚Äôs the habit they have got of speaking of what are called ‚Äòchefs-d‚Äôoeuvre‚Äô with bated breath. The object of The Flat Iron (it‚Äôs to be the name of the review too) is to make this reverence appear grotesque‚ÅÝ‚Äîto discredit it.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Another good plan is to hold up to the reader‚Äôs admiration something absolutely idiotic (my Nocturnal Vase for instance) by an author who is absolutely senseless.‚Äù

‚ÄúDoes Passavant approve of all this?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe‚Äôs very much amused by it.‚Äù

‚ÄúI see I did well to retire.‚Äù

‚ÄúRetire!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Sooner or later, old man, willy-nilly, one always has to end by retiring. This wise reflection naturally leads me to take my leave.‚Äù

‚ÄúStop a moment, you old clown.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ What made you say just now that your father played the part of pastor? Don‚Äôt you think he is in earnest?‚Äù

‚ÄúMy revered father has so arranged his life that he hasn‚Äôt the right now‚ÅÝ‚Äîor even the power‚ÅÝ‚Äînot to be in earnest. Yes, it‚Äôs his profession to be in earnest. He‚Äôs a professor of earnestness. He inculcates faith; it‚Äôs his raison d‚Äô√™tre; it‚Äôs the role he has chosen and he must go through with it to the very end. But as for knowing what goes on in what he calls his ‚Äòinner consciousness‚Äô‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ it would be indiscreet to enquire. And I don‚Äôt think he ever enquires himself. He manages in such a way that he never has time to. He has crammed his life full of a lot of obligations which would lose all meaning if his conviction failed; so that in a manner they necessitate his conviction and at the same time keep it going. He imagines he believes, because he continues to act as if he did. If his faith failed, my dear fellow, why, it would be a catastrophic collapse! And reflect, that at the same time my family would cease to have anything to live on. That‚Äôs a fact that must be taken into consideration, old boy. Papa‚Äôs faith is our means of subsistence. So that to come and ask me if Papa‚Äôs faith is genuine, is not, you must admit, a very tactful proceeding on your part.‚Äù

‚ÄúI thought you lived chiefly on what the school brings in.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes; there‚Äôs some truth in that. But that‚Äôs not very tactful either‚ÅÝ‚Äîto cut me short in my lyrical flights.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd you then? Don‚Äôt you believe in anything?‚Äù asked Olivier sadly, for he was fond of Armand, and his ugliness pained him.

‚ÄúJubes renovare dolorem.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ You seem to forget, my dear friend, that my parents wanted to make a pastor of me. They nourished me on pious precepts‚ÅÝ‚Äîfed me up with them, if I may say so.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ But finally they were obliged to recognize that I hadn‚Äôt the vocation. It‚Äôs a pity. I might have made a first-class preacher. But my vocation was to write The Nocturnal Vase.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou poor old thing! If you knew how sorry I am for you!‚Äù

‚ÄúYou have always had what my father calls ‚Äòa heart of gold‚Äô.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I won‚Äôt trespass on it any longer.‚Äù

He took up his hat. He had almost left the room, when he suddenly turned round:

‚ÄúYou haven‚Äôt asked after Sarah?‚Äù

‚ÄúBecause you could tell me nothing that I haven‚Äôt heard from Bernard.‚Äù

‚ÄúDid he tell you that he had left the pension?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe told me that your sister Rachel had requested him to leave.‚Äù

Armand had one hand on the door handle; with his walking-stick in the other, he pushed up the porti√®re. The stick went into a hole in the porti√®re and made it bigger.

‚ÄúAccount for it how you will,‚Äù said he, and his face became very grave. ‚ÄúRachel is, I believe, the only person in the world I love and respect. I respect her because she is virtuous. And I always behave in such a way as to offend her virtue. As for Bernard and Sarah, she had no suspicions. It was I who told her the whole thing.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ And the oculist said she wasn‚Äôt to cry! It‚Äôs comic!‚Äù

‚ÄúAm I to think you sincere now?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, I think the most sincere thing about me is a horror‚ÅÝ‚Äîa hatred of everything people call ‚ÄòVirtue.‚Äô Don‚Äôt try to understand. You have no idea what a Puritan bringing-up can do to one. It leaves one with an incurable resentment in one‚Äôs heart‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ to judge by myself,‚Äù he added, with a jarring laugh.

He put down his hat and went up to the window. ‚ÄúJust look here; on the inside of my lip?‚Äù

He stooped towards Olivier and lifted up his lip with his finger.

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt see anything.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, you can; there; in the corner.‚Äù

Olivier saw a whitish spot near the corner. A little uneasily: ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs a gumboil,‚Äù he said to reassure Armand.

But Armand shrugged his shoulders.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt talk nonsense‚ÅÝ‚Äîsuch a serious fellow as you! A gumboil‚Äôs soft and it goes away. This is hard and gets larger every week. And it gives me a kind of bad taste in my mouth.‚Äù

‚ÄúHave you had it long?‚Äù

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs more than a month since I first noticed it. But as the chef d‚Äôoeuvre says: ‚ÄòMon mal vient de plus loin.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, old boy, if you‚Äôre anxious about it, you had better consult a doctor.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt suppose I needed your advice for that.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat did he say?‚Äù

‚ÄúI didn‚Äôt need your advice to say to myself that I ought to consult a doctor. But all the same, I didn‚Äôt consult one, because if it‚Äôs what I think, I prefer not to know it.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs idiotic.‚Äù

‚ÄúIsn‚Äôt it stupid? But so human, my friend, so human.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúThe idiotic thing is not to be treated for it.‚Äù

‚ÄúSo that when one is treated, one can always say: ‚ÄòToo late!‚Äô That‚Äôs what Cob-Lafleur expresses so well in one of his poems which you‚Äôll see in the review:

‚ÄòIl faut se rendre √Ý l‚Äô√©vidence;
Car, dans ce bas monde, la danse
Pr√©c√®de souvent la chanson.‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù

‚ÄúOne can make literature out of anything.‚Äù

‚ÄúJust so; out of anything. But, dear friend, it‚Äôs not so easy as all that. Well, goodbye.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Oh! there‚Äôs one thing more I wanted to tell you. I‚Äôve heard from Alexandre.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Yes, you know‚ÅÝ‚Äîmy eldest brother, who ran away to Africa. He began by coming to grief over his business and running through all the money Rachel sent him. He‚Äôs settled now on the banks of the Casamance; and he has written to say that things are doing well and that he‚Äôll soon be able to pay everything back.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat kind of a business?‚Äù

‚ÄúHeaven knows! Rubber, ivory, Negroes perhaps‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ a lot of odds and ends.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ He has asked me to go out to him.‚Äù

‚ÄúWill you go?‚Äù

‚ÄúI would tomorrow, if it weren‚Äôt for my military service. Alexandre is a kind of donkey, something in my style. I think I should get on with him very well.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Here! would you like to see? I‚Äôve got his letter with me.‚Äù

He took an envelope out of his pocket, and several sheets of notepaper out of the envelope; he chose one, and held it out to Olivier.

‚ÄúThere‚Äôs no need to read it all. Begin here.‚Äù

Olivier read:

‚ÄúFor the last fortnight, I have been living in company with a singular individual whom I have taken into my hut. The sun of these parts seems to have touched him in the upper story. I thought at first it was delirium, but there‚Äôs no doubt it‚Äôs just plain madness. This curious young man is about thirty years old, tall, strong, good-looking, and certainly ‚Äòa gentleman,‚Äô to judge from his manners, his language, and his hands, which are too delicate ever to have done any rough work. The strange thing about him is that he thinks himself possessed by the devil‚ÅÝ‚Äîor rather, as far as I can make out, he thinks he is the devil. He must have had some odd adventure or other, for when he is dreaming or half dozing, a state into which he often falls (and then he talks to himself as if I weren‚Äôt there) he continually speaks of hands being cut off, and as at those times he gets extremely excited and rolls his eyes in an alarming manner, I take care that there shall be no weapons within reach. The rest of the time, he is a good fellow and an agreeable companion‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhich I appreciate, as you can imagine, after months of solitude. Besides which, he is of great assistance to me in my work. He never speaks of his past life, so that I can‚Äôt succeed in discovering who he can be. He is particularly interested in plants and insects, and sometimes in his talk shows signs of being remarkably well educated. He seems to like staying with me and doesn‚Äôt speak of leaving; I have decided to let him stay as long as he likes. I was wanting a help; all things considered, he has come just in the nick of time.

‚ÄúA hideous Negro who came up the Casamance with him, and to whom I have talked a little, speaks of a woman who was with him, and who, I gather, must have been drowned in the river one day when their boat upset. I shouldn‚Äôt be surprised to learn that my companion had had a finger in the accident. In this country, if one wants to get rid of anyone, there is a great choice of means, and no one ever asks a question. If one day I learn anything more, I‚Äôll write it to you‚ÅÝ‚Äîor rather I‚Äôll tell you about it when you come out. Yes, I know, there‚Äôs your service.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Well, I‚Äôll wait. For you may be sure that if ever you want to see me again, you will have to make up your mind to come out. As for me, I want to come back less and less. I lead a life here which I like and which suits me down to the ground. My business is flourishing, and that badge of civilization‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe starched collar‚ÅÝ‚Äîappears to me a straight waistcoat which I shall never be able to endure again.

‚ÄúI enclose a money order which you can do what you like with. The last was for Rachel. Keep this for yourself.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúThe rest isn‚Äôt interesting,‚Äù said Armand.

Olivier gave the letter back without saying anything. It never occurred to him that the murderer it spoke of was his brother. Vincent had given no news of himself for a long time; his parents thought he was in America. To tell the truth, Olivier did not trouble much about him.
